chunks: Polonnaruwa is the second most ancient of Sri Lanka's kingdoms and was the country's capital during the 11th and 12th centuries AD. The ancient city is situated on the south-east bank of the Parakrama Samudra reservoir in the North Central Province of Sri Lanka.

King Vijayabahu I established Polonnaruwa as the capital of Sri Lanka in 1070 AD after defeating the South Indian Chola dynasty that had occupied the island for over 75 years. The city replaced Anuradhapura as the political and cultural centre of the island.

Polonnaruwa reached its golden age under King Parakramabahu I, who reigned from 1153 to 1186 AD. He is famous for the declaration that not a single drop of rain should be allowed to fall into the sea without being made useful to man. He oversaw large-scale construction including palaces, temples, and irrigation systems.

The Gal Vihara at Polonnaruwa is one of the most celebrated rock-cut sculptures in the world. Carved in the 12th century under King Parakramabahu I, it features four colossal Buddha figures cut from a single granite rock face. The figures include a small seated Buddha inside a chamber, a large seated meditating Buddha, a standing 7-metre Buddha, and a 14-metre reclining Parinirvana Buddha.

The Vatadage at Polonnaruwa is a circular relic house or gedige built on a two-tiered decorated terrace. Its central small stupa is surrounded by four seated Buddha statues facing the four cardinal directions. The Vatadage is celebrated for its beautifully carved moonstone threshold and elaborate guard-stone reliefs, among the finest in Sri Lanka.

Rankot Vihara is the largest stupa or dagoba in Polonnaruwa, standing 55 metres high. It was built by King Nissanka Malla between 1187 and 1196 AD in the classic style of the great stupas of Anuradhapura. Its name means Golden Pinnacle Vihara. The stupa is surrounded by a boundary wall with four entrance gopuras or gateway towers.

Lankatilaka is a colossal image house or gedige at Polonnaruwa constructed by King Parakramabahu I in the 12th century. Its brick walls soar over 17 metres high and enclose an 18-metre headless brick Buddha statue. The head of the statue toppled in a historical earthquake. The exterior walls are decorated with detailed bas-relief carvings of divine beings.

The Parakrama Samudra, meaning Sea of Parakrama, is a vast ancient reservoir constructed under King Parakramabahu I. It covers approximately 2,500 hectares and its retaining embankment or bund stretches for 84 kilometres. It is the largest ancient irrigation reservoir in Sri Lanka and still irrigates agricultural land today.

The ancient city of Polonnaruwa was declared a UNESCO World Heritage Site in 1982. It was inscribed along with other monuments of the Cultural Triangle of Sri Lanka, which also includes Sigiriya, Dambulla, Anuradhapura, and Kandy. The site is managed by the Central Cultural Fund of Sri Lanka.

After the reign of Parakramabahu I, successive weak rulers and invasions from South India led to the gradual abandonment of Polonnaruwa as a capital. By the 13th century the capital had shifted southward. The city lay largely forgotten and overgrown by jungle until it was rediscovered and excavated by British archaeologists in the 19th century.

The Royal Palace of Parakramabahu at Polonnaruwa was a five-storey building with 1,000 rooms according to ancient chronicles, though only the lower two storeys of brick walls survive today. The walls are up to 3 metres thick and rise to approximately 9 metres in the surviving sections.

The Alahana Parivena at Polonnaruwa was the major monastic complex of the ancient city, comprising the Rankot Vihara, Lankatilaka, Kiri Vihara, and the Chapter House. It was dedicated to the Mahayana school of Buddhism that King Parakramabahu I promoted.

The Kiri Vihara at Polonnaruwa is a well-preserved stupa near Lankatilaka. Its name meaning Milk White Stupa comes from the fact that its original lime plaster coating is still largely intact, giving it a white appearance. It stands about 25 metres high.

The Nissanka Malla Palace complex at Polonnaruwa includes the Audience Hall, which features a row of stone columns carved in the shape of crocodiles. An inscription by Nissanka Malla records that commoners were not allowed to sit in the audience hall — only the king could sit.

The Polonnaruwa Museum was established adjacent to the heritage site and contains many of the smaller carved artefacts, statues, and inscriptions recovered from the ancient city. Notably it displays the famous bronze statues of Shiva and Parvati that were recovered from the Shiva Devale temples at the site.
